text.title: Sourcing Channels and Pipeline Building
text.summary: Multi-channel sourcing strategy, skills-based hiring, passive candidate outreach, and talent pipeline building to reduce reactive, channel-dependent hiring.

rule.statement: Use at least three sourcing channels per role; over-reliance on a single channel (e.g., job boards) caps the addressable candidate pool and biases toward active seekers.
rule.rationale: LinkedIn data shows 70% of the global workforce is passively open to opportunities; job boards only reach active seekers, typically 30% of the market.

section.title: Channel Selection by Use Case
section.p: LinkedIn: passive candidates, highest effort, highest yield for experienced roles. Employee referrals: culture-fit signal, lowest sourcing cost, fastest time-to-screen. Job boards: active seekers, medium effort, works for high-volume or well-known roles. University partnerships: entry-level pipeline, medium effort, requires relationship investment. Niche communities and Slack groups: specialized roles (e.g., ML engineers, security researchers). Agencies: hard-to-fill or time-sensitive roles; significant cost premium.

rule.statement: Remove degree requirements that are not job-essential; replace with skills assessments or portfolio evaluation.
rule.rationale: 76% of organizations report successful hires from candidates previously excluded by degree requirements (LinkedIn). Degree proxies exclude qualified candidates from under-resourced educational backgrounds without improving quality-of-hire.

list.item: Identify the 5-7 essential skills for the role (not proxies).
Remove degree requirements that are not legally mandated.
Create or source skills assessments tied to actual job tasks.
Evaluate portfolio, open-source contributions, or past projects as evidence.
Use structured interviews to assess skills not visible in the portfolio.

section.title: Passive Candidate Outreach

rule.statement: Each outreach message must reference the candidate's specific work; generic "exciting opportunity" subject lines achieve less than 10% open rate.
rule.rationale: Passive candidates receive multiple generic outreach messages weekly. A specific reference to a project, article, or contribution they produced proves the sender did research and respects their time.

section.p: Target response rate: 25-40%. Outreach structure: personalized subject (no "job opportunity"), opening referencing their specific work, value proposition framed for what they gain, low-commitment CTA (a conversation, not an application). Follow-up: 3 touches with different angles, then stop.

rule.statement: Build and maintain warm candidate lists for critical roles before those roles open; reactive hiring from a cold start adds 2-4 weeks to time-to-fill.
rule.rationale: Pipeline building converts recruiting from a reactive sprint into an ongoing relationship. When a role opens, the first outreach goes to candidates who already know the company.

list.item: Identify 3-5 roles most likely to open in the next 2 quarters.
Build a warm list of 20-50 candidates per role.
Nurture with content, company updates, and event invitations — not job postings.
Activate the warm list within 24 hours of role opening.

rule.statement: Post all roles internally before external sourcing begins; an internal hire closes 50-70% faster and retains institutional knowledge.
rule.rationale: Internal candidates require less ramp time, already understand culture, and their move opens a backfill at a more junior level — cheaper to fill. Skipping internal posting destroys trust in career development commitments.
